source.submodule: Math Operators and overall code structure

topicTags: Math Operators and overall code structure

content.problemStatement:
Let us now look at some of the nuts and bolts of C#. In particular, what is our syntax mean and what it does?

- 1): *using System;*
  - This line is an import statement, which allows you to use types and members from the *System* namespace in your code without having to provide the fully qualified names. The *System* namespace contains fundamental types and classes used in C# programs.

- 2): *namespace MyApplication*
  -  This is a namespace declaration. Namespaces are used to organize code and prevent naming conflicts. In this case, the code is declaring a namespace called *MyApplication*, which contains the rest of the code in the program.

- 3): *class Program*
  - This is a class declaration. Classes are fundamental building blocks in C# and are used to define objects with properties and methods. In this case, the class is named *Program*.

- 4): *static void Main(string[] args)*
  - This is the main entry point for the program. When the application starts, it begins executing from this method. The *Main* method has the *static* modifier, meaning it belongs to the class itself rather than an instance of the class. It takes a string array *args* as a parameter, which allows you to pass command-line arguments to the program.
  - Don't worry if you didn't understand it, the more you learn about it, the better you will get with it.

- 5): *Console.Write()*
  - This is a method in C# that allows you to write text or other data to the standard output stream without a newline character.

- 6): *{ }*
  - These curly braces define a code block, also known as a method body. You can add your code inside the Main method to perform various tasks when the program runs.

**Try displaying the sum of 12 & 7 using the given code**
constraints:
- $1 \leq T \leq 100$
- $1 \leq N \leq 100$
- $1 \leq M \leq N\cdot(N-1)/2$
- $1 \leq u_i, v_i \leq N$
- $u_i \neq v_i$ for each $1 \leq i \leq M$.
- The sum of $N$ over all test cases won't exceed $100$.
content.inputFormat:
- The first line of input will contain a single integer $T$, denoting the number of test cases.
- Each test case consists of multiple lines of input.
    - The first line of each test case contains two space-separated integers $N$ and $M$ — the number of vertices and edges of the graph, respectively.
    - The next $M$ lines describe the edges. The $i$-th of these $M$ lines contains two space-separated integers $u_i$ and $v_i$, denoting an edge between $u_i$ and $v_i$.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, output on a new line the number of good subgraphs of $G$, modulo $10^9 + 7$.
